content.text: FinOps KPI Tracking and Improvement Targets

FinOps KPIs for Claude spend are measurable signals that tell the program whether it is working. Tracking them over time distinguishes genuine improvement from noise, and setting improvement targets gives teams a concrete goal to work toward each quarter. This document defines the canonical KPI set, organises it by maturity level, provides the SQL formulas and Python code to compute each metric, and explains how to set and track quarterly improvement targets.

attrs.alt: FinOps KPI maturity ladder: four levels from basic spend visibility to AI ROI and automated anomaly detection

content.text: FinOps programs do not start with sophisticated unit economics metrics. They start by getting spend visible. The maturity ladder sequences KPI adoption so each level builds on the previous one. A team at Level 1 should not try to compute AI ROI — the data quality required for that metric doesn't exist yet.
Total spend, spend by team, budget vs actual
Usage logging in place; invoices reconciled
Cost per conversation, cache hit rate, month-end forecast
Unit economics margin, batch utilization, action realisation rate
AI ROI, anomaly detection P95, forecast accuracy
Business value metrics instrumented; ML anomaly model

Most teams are at Level 1 or 2 after the first six months. Level 3 requires completed playbook implementations. Level 4 requires connecting Claude costs to revenue or business outcomes — data that many teams don't have yet. Set targets at the level you're actually at, not at where you want to be.

Every KPI must have an unambiguous formula that produces the same number regardless of who runs the query. Ambiguity in the formula is the most common cause of disagreements between teams and FinOps about whether costs are 'really' going up.

# kpi_definitions.py
# Canonical KPI definitions for Claude FinOps.
# All formulas use the same field names as the usage_events table.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class KPI:
    name: str
    description: str
    formula: str             # human-readable formula
    unit: str
    target_direction: str    # "lower" | "higher"
    green_threshold: float   # >= this is green (for "higher"), <= for "lower"
    red_threshold: float     # below this is red (for "higher"), above for "lower"
    level: int               # 1=Crawl, 2=Walk, 3=Run, 4=Fly
    compute: Callable        # callable(db_row_or_dict) -> float

KPIS: list[KPI] = [
    KPI(
        name="monthly_spend_usd",
        description="Total Claude API cost for the month, all teams.",
        formula="SUM(cost_usd) WHERE month = current_month",
        unit="USD",
        target_direction="lower",
        green_threshold=0,   # n/a — compare vs budget
        red_threshold=float("inf"),
        level=1,
        compute=lambda rows: sum(r["cost_usd"] for r in rows),
    ),
    KPI(
        name="budget_utilization_pct",
        description="Actual spend as a percentage of approved monthly budget.",
        formula="mtd_spend / monthly_budget * 100",
        unit="%",
        target_direction="lower",
        green_threshold=0,
        red_threshold=100,   # over 100% = red
        level=1,
        compute=lambda r: r["mtd_spend"] / r["budget"] * 100,
    ),
    KPI(
        name="cost_per_conversation",
        description="Average cost per end-user interaction. Tracks efficiency over time.",
        formula="total_cost_usd / request_count",
        unit="USD/request",
        target_direction="lower",
        green_threshold=0.0,
        red_threshold=0.10,  # > $0.10/req triggers review
        level=2,
        compute=lambda r: r["total_cost"] / max(r["request_count"], 1),
    ),
    KPI(
        name="cache_hit_rate",
        description="Fraction of input tokens served from cache. Proxy for prompt engineering quality.",
        formula="cache_read_input_tokens / total_input_tokens",
        unit="%",
        target_direction="higher",
        green_threshold=0.70,   # >= 70% is green
        red_threshold=0.40,     # < 40% is red
        level=2,
        compute=lambda r: r["cache_read_tokens"] / max(r["input_tokens"], 1),
    ),
    KPI(
        name="batch_utilization_pct",
        description="Fraction of batch-eligible requests routed to the Batch API.",
        formula="batch_requests / batch_eligible_requests * 100",
        unit="%",
        target_direction="higher",
        green_threshold=0.80,
        red_threshold=0.30,
        level=3,
        compute=lambda r: r["batch_requests"] / max(r["batch_eligible"], 1),
    ),
    KPI(
        name="action_realisation_rate",
        description="Actual savings delivered by completed actions vs projected savings.",
        formula="actual_saving_usd / expected_saving_usd * 100",
        unit="%",
        target_direction="higher",
        green_threshold=0.80,
        red_threshold=0.50,
        level=3,
        compute=lambda r: r["actual_saving"] / max(r["expected_saving"], 0.01),
    ),
    KPI(
        name="forecast_accuracy_pct",
        description="How close the mid-month linear forecast was to actual month-end spend.",
        formula="1 - abs(forecast - actual) / actual",
        unit="%",
        target_direction="higher",
        green_threshold=0.90,
        red_threshold=0.75,
        level=4,
        compute=lambda r: 1 - abs(r["forecast"] - r["actual"]) / max(r["actual"], 0.01),
    ),
]

The threshold values in the KPI definitions are defaults, not requirements. Adjust them based on your organization's risk tolerance and cost structure. A startup running Claude at $5K/month has a different acceptable 'red' threshold for cost-per-conversation than an enterprise running at $500K/month.
The tracker stores monthly KPI snapshots and computes trends across them. Trend direction — is cache hit rate improving or worsening? — is the signal that drives priority for the next review cycle.

# kpi_tracker.py
# Track KPI values over time and detect improvement or regression.

import json
import datetime
from pathlib import Path

def record_kpi_snapshot(
    tracker_path: str,
    period: str,         # "2026-07"
    kpi_values: dict,    # {kpi_name: value}
) -> None:
    """Append a monthly KPI snapshot to the tracker file."""
    path = Path(tracker_path)
    history = json.loads(path.read_text()) if path.exists() else []
    history.append({"period": period, "recorded_at": datetime.date.today().isoformat(), **kpi_values})
    path.write_text(json.dumps(history, indent=2))


def compute_trends(history: list[dict], kpi_name: str, lookback: int = 3) -> dict:
    """
    Compute the trend for a KPI over the last N periods.
    Returns direction, magnitude, and whether the trend is improving.
    """
    values = [h[kpi_name] for h in history[-lookback:] if kpi_name in h]
    if len(values) < 2:
        return {"trend": "insufficient_data"}
    delta = values[-1] - values[0]
    pct_change = delta / abs(values[0]) * 100 if values[0] else 0
    return {
        "start_value": values[0],
        "end_value": values[-1],
        "delta": round(delta, 4),
        "pct_change": round(pct_change, 1),
        "trend": "improving" if delta < 0 else "worsening",  # lower = better for cost KPIs
    }


def generate_kpi_summary(history: list[dict], kpis: list[str]) -> str:
    """Generate a one-paragraph trend summary for the monthly FinOps report."""
    lines = []
    for kpi in kpis:
        trend = compute_trends(history, kpi)
        if trend.get("trend") == "insufficient_data":
            continue
        direction = "improved" if trend["trend"] == "improving" else "worsened"
        lines.append(
            f"{kpi.replace('_', ' ').title()} {direction} "
            f"by {abs(trend['pct_change']):.1f}% over the last 3 months "
            f"({trend['start_value']:.3f} → {trend['end_value']:.3f})."
        )
    return " ".join(lines)
Rising while request count is flat (prompt bloat or model drift)
Falling — system prompt may have changed or TTL is expiring
Flat at 0% after month 3 — batch not being adopted
Falling — estimates are optimistic or implementations are incomplete

Targets should be specific, measurable, and achievable within the quarter given the team's current engineering bandwidth. A team that is also shipping a major product release cannot also fully implement prompt caching and batch routing. Set one or two targets per quarter, not five.

# improvement_targets.py
# Define quarterly improvement targets and check whether the team is on track.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class ImprovementTarget:
    kpi_name: str
    baseline: float      # value at the start of the quarter
    target: float        # value we want by end of quarter
    current: float       # latest measured value
    quarter: str         # "Q3-2026"

    def progress_pct(self) -> float:
        """How far along the improvement have we come? 100% = target reached."""
        if self.baseline == self.target:
            return 100.0
        return (self.current - self.baseline) / (self.target - self.baseline) * 100

    def on_track(self, weeks_elapsed: int, weeks_in_quarter: int = 13) -> bool:
        """True if progress is at least proportional to time elapsed."""
        time_pct = weeks_elapsed / weeks_in_quarter * 100
        return self.progress_pct() >= time_pct * 0.8   # allow 20% lag


# Example Q3 targets for a team starting the FinOps program:
EXAMPLE_TARGETS = [
    ImprovementTarget(
        kpi_name="cache_hit_rate",
        baseline=0.42,   # 42% at start of Q3
        target=0.70,     # 70% by end of Q3
        current=0.58,    # current (week 6 of 13)
        quarter="Q3-2026",
    ),
    ImprovementTarget(
        kpi_name="cost_per_conversation",
        baseline=0.045,  # $0.045 at start of Q3
        target=0.030,    # $0.030 by end of Q3
        current=0.038,   # current
        quarter="Q3-2026",
    ),
    ImprovementTarget(
        kpi_name="batch_utilization_pct",
        baseline=0.0,    # not using Batch API yet
        target=0.60,     # 60% of eligible requests batched
        current=0.25,    # in progress
        quarter="Q3-2026",
    ),
]

def print_target_report(targets: list[ImprovementTarget], week: int) -> None:
    print(f"{'KPI':<28} {'Baseline':>10} {'Current':>10} {'Target':>10} {'Progress%':>10} {'On Track':>10}")
    print("-" * 82)
    for t in targets:
        status = "YES" if t.on_track(week) else "NO ⚠"
        print(
            f"{t.kpi_name:<28} {t.baseline:>10.3f} {t.current:>10.3f} "
            f"{t.target:>10.3f} {t.progress_pct():>9.1f}% {status:>10}"
        )

# print_target_report(EXAMPLE_TARGETS, week=6)
# KPI                          Baseline    Current     Target  Progress%   On Track
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# cache_hit_rate                  0.420      0.580      0.700     57.1%        YES
# cost_per_conversation           0.045      0.038      0.030     46.7%         NO ⚠
# batch_utilization_pct           0.000      0.250      0.600     41.7%        YES
Measure the current KPI value for at least 4 weeks before setting a target. A single week's number is unreliable.
Set targets at 60–80% of theoretical maximum:
Cache hit rate peaks around 85% for most workloads. A target of 90% is unachievable and demoralising.
One hard target, one stretch target per quarter:
The hard target is the minimum acceptable improvement. The stretch target is what a great quarter looks like.
If progress is below 40% at the midpoint, the target may need to be revised or the implementation plan accelerated.

At the end of each month, the FinOps program generates a narrative summary that goes to the VP of Engineering. The summary is three paragraphs: headline achievement, where we fell short, and the focus for next month. Claude generates the draft from the structured KPI data.

# monthly_kpi_report.py
# Generate the monthly FinOps KPI narrative for the executive email.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def generate_monthly_narrative(
    current_kpis: dict,
    prior_kpis: dict,
    targets: list[dict],  # [{kpi_name, target, current, progress_pct}]
) -> str:
    """
    Generate a 3-paragraph monthly narrative:
    1. Headline achievement
    2. Where we fell short and why
    3. Focus for next month
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=400,
        system=(
            "You are a FinOps program manager writing the monthly KPI summary "
            "for the VP of Engineering. Be specific about numbers. "
            "Use three paragraphs: achievement, shortfall, next month focus. "
            "No bullet points. Professional but direct tone."
        ),
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Current KPIs: {json.dumps(current_kpis)}\n"
                f"Prior month: {json.dumps(prior_kpis)}\n"
                f"Quarterly targets: {json.dumps(targets)}"
            ),
        }],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

The generated narrative is a first draft. The FinOps lead reviews it for accuracy and adds context that the model cannot know — a product launch that temporarily inflated costs, or a team that deliberately increased batch usage after a pricing conversation. Never send the generated draft directly without review.
A rising cost-per-conversation during user growth is a success story, not a failure. Always track cost alongside usage volume.
Organisations that only track actuals are always reacting. The forecast KPI is the only one that gives time to act before overspend occurs.
Using mean instead of median for cost per conversation:
A handful of very expensive requests (long documents, image processing) skew the mean. Track p50 and p95 separately.
A cache hit rate target of 70% means nothing without identifying which services will be cached and by when.
Conflating team spend with team cost efficiency:
The highest-spending team may also have the highest ROI. Spend level is not itself a KPI — spend efficiency is.

FinOps KPIs for Claude spend are organised into four maturity levels: visibility (spend, budget), efficiency (cost per conversation, cache hit rate), optimization (batch utilization, action realisation), and intelligence (AI ROI, forecast accuracy). Start at Level 1 and advance only after the current level's KPIs are stable and well-understood. Set one or two quarterly improvement targets per team with explicit baselines and implementation plans. Track trend direction monthly — a consistently improving cache hit rate is more meaningful than any single month's value. The monthly narrative report closes the loop by summarising achievements and setting the focus for the next cycle.
